questions.description: Carol is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and… Advise Carol.
questions.text: Carol is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. Carol receives a phone call from a local mushroom grower, Fred, asking if Carol would be interested in taking a truck full of mushroom compost for use in her business, as otherwise he "would just be throwing it out". Carol is delighted and immediately agrees to take the compost. When she collects the compost the next day Carol is so happy with it that she promises she will pay him €200. Fred is delighted by this; however, Carol never pays him the money. Fred is now threatening to sue Carol for the money. In addition, Carol decides that she needs to buy a small digger for her business. On Monday, she emails Niamh, the owner of "Garden Equipment Ltd", to enquire about the possibility of buying a "Micro-Digger". That afternoon, Niamh responds as follows: "Thank you for your enquiry. We have one Micro-Digger for sale, at a cost of €10,000 including VAT and delivery charges. We can keep it for you until the weekend, so please let us know by then if you wish to purchase." On Friday Carol replies, saying "That's great, I'll take it." However, Niamh replies and says that she sold the Micro-Digger to somebody else on Wednesday and so she cannot sell it to Carol. Carol is angry because she cannot find the digger for sale at this price anywhere else, and she believes she has a binding contract with Niamh. Advise Carol.

questions.description: John goes to his local cinema approximately once a month. Negligenc, Conditions Of Sale.
questions.text: John goes to his local cinema approximately once a month. He usually pays for his ticket in advance on the cinema website, and collects the ticket from a machine in the cinema foyer. When he purchases the ticket on the website, he always ticks a box that says he has "read and agreed to the terms and conditions of sale". These are available through a hyperlink to another part of the cinema website, but he has never in fact read these terms. Last Saturday, John bought his ticket online as per usual. However, when he was in the cinema he tripped on an electric cable which had been left in the cinema aisle by a member of staff. He badly injured his back, and wants to bring an action against the cinema for negligence. However, Carla, the manager of the cinema, claims that John cannot bring an action against the cinema in either contract or tort because of clause 12 of the Terms and Conditions of Sale, as available on the website. Clause 12 states: "The cinema is not liable in contract, tort or any other action for any damage to property or persons howsoever caused while on the premises." Advise John on whether clause 12 has been incorporated into his contract, and, assuming it is incorporated, whether it can prevent him from bringing an action against the cinema. Refer to any applicable consumer legislation in your answer. Please note there is no need to discuss the law of negligence in your answer.

questions.description: In March 2018, Peter was contacted by Elaine, an employee of Invest Ltd,… Advise Peter.
questions.text: In March 2018, Peter was contacted by Elaine, an employee of Invest Ltd, about an investment opportunity being offered to clients of her firm. Elaine sent Peter a 7 page brochure regarding a property investment fund which was seeking investors for a shopping centre in Cork. This brochure stated that the redeveloped shopping centre would provide 120,000 square metres of retail space, and that planning permission was available for a hotel on the land. In April 2018 Peter agreed to invest €1 million in the property fund. The formal contractual documentation made no mention of the amount of retail space or the planning permission for a hotel. However, in May 2018 Peter was told that in fact only 90,000 square meters of retail space would be available. In addition, it transpired that although planning permission had been available in March 2018, it had expired at the start of April 2018 and further applications would be necessary to build a hotel. Peter regrets investing in the shopping centre and wishes to get his money back. However, when he contacts Elaine to complain she tells him that he "should have done his due diligence and checked the facts and figures before he invested". Advise Peter.

questions.description: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to hotels and businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refrigerated trucks to make deliveries. In April 2018, Coldstore ltd entered into a contract with Bliss Hotel, where it agreed to deliver a week's supply of frozen and refrigerated food to the hotel every Monday for a period of one year. However, one week in May 2018, due to a fault in the truck's refrigeration system, the food was considered unsafe for consumption on arrival at the hotel and had to be thrown out. As a result, the hotel restaurant had to close for a week. Coldstore Ltd accepted that it was in breach of contract, and agreed to refund the price of the wasted food. However, Bliss Hotels is claiming damages for the lost profits from the closure of the restaurant. Coldstore ltd believes that it is not responsible for this loss, and that Bliss Hotel could have mitigated its loss by purchasing in other sources of food. In May 2018, Coldstore Ltd also agreed to be a transporter for a large pharmaceutical company, Xyntath Ltd. Coldstore Ltd's manager Tom signed the pharmaceutical company's standard form contract. In June 2018, one of the deliveries was two days late due to a shortage of drivers. Xyntath Ltd is claiming damages and is seeking to rely on the following clause of its contract with Coldstore Ltd: "In the event that any delivery is more than 24 hours late, the transporter shall be responsible for the costs of all and any delays in production and a payment of 120% of the cost of the material transported shall be immediately due." Coldstore Ltd believes that this is a penalty clause and should not be enforceable. Advise Coldstore Ltd.

questions.description: What approach do courts take when interpreting contractual documents?
questions.text: What approach do courts take when interpreting contractual documents? Refer to cases in your answer. Please note that you are not required to discuss the parol evidence rule in your answer.

questions.description: Explain the difference between a condition, a warranty and an innominate…
questions.text: Explain the difference between a condition, a warranty and an innominate term. Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: Discuss the relationship between the doctrines of consideration and promissory estoppel.
questions.text: Discuss the relationship between the doctrines of consideration and promissory estoppel.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss the circumstances in which a contract will be declared invalid on the grounds of economic…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss the circumstances in which a contract will be declared invalid on the grounds of economic duress. Refer to cases in your answer. [Or]{.underline} (b) Which contractual principles are used by the courts to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to financial institutions that advance monies to close friends and family members of the guarantor? Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: Ben owns a business which produces organic fruit juices and drinks. Breach Of Contract, Conditions Of Sale.
questions.text: Ben owns a business which produces organic fruit juices and drinks. He emails Joe, a local farmer, offering to buy 100 crates of organic strawberries for supply the following month, to be delivered in four weekly instalments. Joe replies, saying "Yes, that should be no problem. Just fill out and return the order form attached to this email and we will deliver." Ben fills out the order form, typing his name at the bottom of the first page of the order form where it says "signature", and emails it back to Joe. The first page of the order form says "All orders are subject to the terms and conditions of sale". These terms and conditions of sale are on the second page of the order form, but Ben does not read them. One term states as follows: "In the event of any fruit sold not complying with the express terms of the contract of sale or any statutory warranties, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation. We hereby exclude all liability for any direct or indirect loss or damage arising from the use of any fruit supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use." These terms and conditions are also contained in the delivery docket which Ben signs when the fruit is delivered each week. However, in the fourth week Joe inadvertently sprays his fruit crop with chemical pesticides. When Ben uses this fruit a week's production is contaminated and cannot be sold as "organic" juice. When Ben sues Joe for breach of contract, Joe seeks to rely on the limitation clause. Ben argues that the limitation clause is not incorporated in the contract or alternatively that it is not effective to protect Joe. Advise Ben.

questions.description: In 2015 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with… Consideration, Implied Term.
questions.text: In 2015 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with Max, the owner of a large premises, which PE Ltd planned to use as a pub and nightclub. However, in January 2018 owing to construction works causing disruption in the area, PE Ltd was struggling to pay the agreed rent. Max agreed to accept a reduced rent from PE Ltd, "for as long as construction works continue". There was no consideration for the promise to accept a lower rent, nor was the promise formalised in a deed under seal. By June 2019 the construction works were finished and PE Ltd began paying the higher rate of rent again. However, Max is now claiming payment of the unpaid portion of the original rate of rent for the period from January 2018 - May 2019. In addition, since 2015 PE Ltd has regularly ordered champagne from Bubbly Ltd. They have no written supply contract. Each time PE Ltd needs a delivery they simply contact Bubbly Ltd and order what they need. This occurs approximately once a month. However, PE Ltd decide that they want to focus more on craft beers, and mention to Bubble Ltd that they will not be placing further orders . Bubbly Ltd however claims that PE Ltd cannot simply terminate their "arrangement" without notice, and that there is an implied term that at least six months' notice should be given of any intention to terminate the contract. PE Ltd claims there is no such contract or implied term. Advise PE Ltd.

questions.description: Aoife owns an outdoor forest adventure centre for children. Company.
questions.text: Aoife owns an outdoor forest adventure centre for children. She advertises the sale of the business and premises in the local newspaper. Sam is looking to open up a small business in the local area and, seeing the advertisement, he visits the premises. Aoife tells him that the profits of the business are usually about €30,000 per year. She offers Sam the opportunity to look at the business accounts, but Sam says he will take her word for it because he is in a hurry. The next day, Aoife's insurance company contacts her to say that owing to increased claims in the sector that they will be increasing the insurance premiums for all adventure centres by 40%. Aoife decides not to tell Sam as she does not want to risk him deciding to not buy the business. The following week Sam enters into a written contract to purchase the business and premises for €200,000. The contract makes no mention of the profit figures or the availability of insurance. A week after completion of the sale, Sam discovers that profits had not exceeded €20,000 in the last three years. In addition, when he attempts to get insurance he finds that the premiums are so excessive that the profits are likely to be greatly reduced this year. As a result Sam wishes he had never bought the premises and business. Advise Sam on whether he has any cause of action against Aoife and on the remedies that may be available to him.

questions.description: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the… Advise Calm.
questions.text: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is in ill health and quite dependent on his neighbour, Sheila, who regularly visits him and assists him with basic household jobs such as cooking and cleaning. One day Sheila approaches Jack and asks him to sign a petition to stop the closure of a local school. Jack doesn't have his reading glasses and so he signs the petition without reading it. Jack's nephew Calm visits Jack later that year and upon investigation discovers that the "petition" is, in fact, a bank guarantee form which guarantees Sheila's debts up to €25,000. Calm is concerned that his uncle may become liable under this guarantee and wants to know if there is any way the guarantee may be void. When Calm is visiting his uncle he attends a local car auction. He bids €5,000 for a car which is advertised as "without reserve". However, this is the only bid and the car is withdrawn from the auction by Audrey, on the instructions of the owner of the car. That evening Calm sees the car for sale on a car sales website for €9,000. Colm wishes to know if he has a binding agreement to buy the car for €5,000. Advise Calm.

questions.description: Analyse the approach of the Irish courts to the duty of a plaintiff to… Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Analyse the approach of the Irish courts to the duty of a plaintiff to mitigate loss caused by a breach of contract. Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: Explain what is meant by the phrases "repudiatory breach", "anticipatory breach" and "breach of an innominate term".
questions.text: Explain what is meant by the phrases "repudiatory breach", "anticipatory breach" and "breach of an innominate term". Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) (a) By reference to recent case law explain what are the basic rules by which Irish judges will…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) (a) By reference to recent case law explain what are the basic rules by which Irish judges will decide on the meaning to be given to the express terms of a written contract. (b) By reference to recent case law summarise the sources to which Irish Judges may resort when deciding if a contract is to be held to be subject to an implied term.
